<!-- PROSE — edit freely in Obsidian -->

*Bethink Yourselves!* (Одумайтесь!; 1904) is an essay by [[Leo Tolstoy]] written in May 1904, at the outset of the Russo-Japanese War, and first published in *The Times* (London). It is one of the most sustained anti-war pieces Tolstoy produced and extends the moral arguments of [[The Kingdom of God Is Within You]] into the specific circumstances of the 1904 conflict.

The essay is structured in twelve numbered sections. In the opening sections Tolstoy argues that the war is a moral impossibility for people who have absorbed Christian teaching, and anatomises the attitudes of soldiers, officers, diplomats, journalists, and the Tsar himself, showing each uses an appeal to abstract collective duty — faith, fatherland, oath — to avoid confronting the personal question of whether he himself should participate in killing. In the central sections he calls for each individual to interrupt his activity and ask who he is and what his destiny is — the "bethinking" of the title, drawn from a Greek word (*metanoeite*) used by John the Baptist and Jesus. This individual moral reckoning, he argues, is the only realistic path to ending war, since all political, diplomatic, and institutional solutions have failed.

Sections XI and XII were added after the main essay was completed. In XI, prompted by news of the sinking of a Russian ship with six hundred lives off Port Arthur, Tolstoy attacks the Tsar and the military command by name, calling the war a needless crime perpetrated by those who "possess inexhaustible human material." He closes with two letters from ordinary men — a reservist and a seaman — that illustrate the gap between what the government says the people feel and what they actually feel.

The essay was translated by [[Vladimir Chertkov]] (identified in the text as "V. Tchertkoff") and an unnamed co-translator ("I. F. M."), and published in London, where Chertkov ran the Free Age Press. It was not legally published in Russia during Tolstoy's lifetime. The Project Gutenberg edition (PG #27189) is a reprint of the pamphlet edition published for the International Union by Ginn & Company, Boston, 1904.
